For confirmed Hypogonadism (Low Testosterone in Men), choose therapy based on severity, comorbidities, pregnancy/fertility context, contraindications, patient preference, and local specialty pathway.
Monitor treatment response with the condition-specific clinical endpoint plus the most relevant labs or imaging listed in this workup.
Escalate to endocrinology or the relevant specialty when results are severe, discordant, recurrent, treatment-limiting, or require localization, surgery, genetic testing, or high-risk medication.

Key thresholds and interpretation caveats: Prolactin upper limit often about 20 ng/mL in men and 25 ng/mL in women, but lab, pregnancy, lactation, and macroprolactin matter. Male total testosterone should be early morning and repeated; lower limit often around 264-300 ng/dL depending assay.

Important mimics/exclusions: pregnancy, hypothalamic/pituitary disease, thyroid disease, hyperprolactinemia, PCOS, androgen-secreting tumor, medication or substance effects, primary gonadal failure, menopause/POI, and structural pelvic or testicular disease.
Fertility plans change away from exogenous testosterone; pituitary signs trigger MRI/endocrine referral.
Unstable physiology, endocrine crisis, pregnancy-critical medication issue, or dangerous electrolyte/glucose abnormality changes disposition to urgent monitored care.
Discordant or borderline endocrine tests should be confirmed and interpreted with timing, assay, medication, pregnancy, and acute illness context before irreversible treatment.
Pregnancy, fertility intent, rapid virilization, tumor-range hormones, or pituitary symptoms changes medication safety, imaging urgency, and referral pathway.

criteria_options.label: Testosterone therapy safety stop/review: PSA >4 ng/mL, PSA >3 ng/mL in high-risk men without urology review, hematocrit >54%, or MI/stroke within last 6 months
criteria_options.criteria_text: Endocrine Society recommends against starting testosterone therapy with prostate cancer risk findings including PSA >4 ng/mL or >3 ng/mL in high-risk men without urologic evaluation, elevated hematocrit; trials defined erythrocytosis as hematocrit >54%, and MI/stroke within 6 months is a contraindication.

criteria_options.label: Male hypogonadism pituitary imaging trigger: severe secondary hypogonadism with serum testosterone <150 ng/dL, persistent hyperprolactinemia, panhypopituitarism, or mass effect
criteria_options.criteria_text: Endocrine Society suggests pituitary imaging when severe secondary hypogonadism is present, for example serum testosterone <150 ng/dL, or with panhypopituitarism, persistent hyperprolactinemia, or headache/visual field mass effect.
criteria_options.source_section: Figure 1 diagnostic evaluation

criteria_options.label: Testosterone therapy safety stop/review: PSA >4 ng/mL, PSA >3 ng/mL in high-risk men without urology review, hematocrit >54%, or MI/stroke within last 6 months
criteria_options.criteria_text: Endocrine Society recommends against starting testosterone therapy with prostate cancer risk findings including PSA >4 ng/mL or >3 ng/mL in high-risk men without urologic evaluation, elevated hematocrit; trials defined erythrocytosis as hematocrit >54%, and MI/stroke within 6 months is a contraindication.
criteria_options.source_section: Treatment contraindications and systematic review harms

criteria_options.label: Testosterone therapy safety stop/review: PSA >4 ng/mL, PSA >3 ng/mL in high-risk men without urology review, hematocrit >54%, or MI/stroke within last 6 months
criteria_options.criteria_text: Endocrine Society recommends against starting testosterone therapy with prostate cancer risk findings including PSA >4 ng/mL or >3 ng/mL in high-risk men without urologic evaluation, elevated hematocrit; trials defined erythrocytosis as hematocrit >54%, and MI/stroke within 6 months is a contraindication.

children.label: Hypogonadism (Low Testosterone in Men): emergency escalation or monitored-care disposition when danger thresholds are met
children.edgeLabel: Hypogonadism (Low Testosterone in Men) danger feature or high-risk cutoff: New severe headache/vision loss, very low T with low/normal LH/FSH, testicular mass, infertility priority.; Hemodynamic instability, altered mental status, severe electrolyte/glucose abnormality, arrhythmia, airway compromise, or vision threat requires urgent escalation.; Positive pregnancy with pain/bleeding, rapid virilization, tumor-range androgens, severe abnormal bleeding, visual symptoms/headache, testicular mass, hard breast mass, or priapism.; Measure blood pressure; <150 ng/dL; >4 ng/mL; >3 ng/mL; Fertility plans change away from exogenous testosterone; pituitary signs trigger MRI/endocrine referral.

criteria.description: Escalate Hypogonadism (Low Testosterone in Men) when any condition-specific danger feature, high-risk cutoff, unstable physiology, or monitored-care disposition rule is present.

criteria_options.label: Male hypogonadism pituitary imaging trigger: severe secondary hypogonadism with serum testosterone <150 ng/dL, persistent hyperprolactinemia, panhypopituitarism, or mass effect
criteria_options.criteria_text: Endocrine Society suggests pituitary imaging when severe secondary hypogonadism is present, for example serum testosterone <150 ng/dL, or with panhypopituitarism, persistent hyperprolactinemia, or headache/visual field mass effect.
criteria_options.source_section: Figure 1 diagnostic evaluation

criteria_options.label: Testosterone therapy safety stop/review: PSA >4 ng/mL, PSA >3 ng/mL in high-risk men without urology review, hematocrit >54%, or MI/stroke within last 6 months
criteria_options.criteria_text: Endocrine Society recommends against starting testosterone therapy with prostate cancer risk findings including PSA >4 ng/mL or >3 ng/mL in high-risk men without urologic evaluation, elevated hematocrit; trials defined erythrocytosis as hematocrit >54%, and MI/stroke within 6 months is a contraindication.
criteria_options.source_section: Treatment contraindications and systematic review harms

children.action: New severe headache/vision loss, very low T with low/normal LH/FSH, testicular mass, infertility priority.; Hemodynamic instability, altered mental status, severe electrolyte/glucose abnormality, arrhythmia, airway compromise, or vision threat requires urgent escalation.; Positive pregnancy with pain/bleeding, rapid virilization, tumor-range androgens, severe abnormal bleeding, visual symptoms/headache, testicular mass, hard breast mass, or priapism.; Measure blood pressure. Treat reversible causes such as obesity, sleep apnea, medications, opioids/anabolic steroid withdrawal, systemic illness, pituitary disease, or hyperprolactinemia.; Use testosterone therapy only for confirmed symptomatic testosterone deficiency without contraindications and with monitoring of symptoms, testosterone level, hematocrit, PSA/prostate risk, and adverse effects.; Use fertility-preserving approaches such as gonadotropins or selective estrogen receptor modulators under specialist care when conception is desired.; Refer severe secondary hypogonadism, pituitary mass symptoms, very low testosterone with low/normal gonadotropins, infertility, or testicular mass urgently.; Fertility plans change away from exogenous testosterone; pituitary signs trigger MRI/endocrine referral.; Unstable physiology, endocrine crisis, pregnancy-critical medication issue, or dangerous electrolyte/glucose abnormality changes disposition to urgent monitored care.; Discordant or borderline endocrine tests should be confirmed and interpreted with timing, assay, medication, pregnancy, and acute illness context before irreversible treatment.

guideline_cutoffs.label: Male hypogonadism pituitary imaging trigger: severe secondary hypogonadism with serum testosterone <150 ng/dL, persistent hyperprolactinemia, panhypopituitarism, or mass effect
guideline_cutoffs.criteria_text: Endocrine Society suggests pituitary imaging when severe secondary hypogonadism is present, for example serum testosterone <150 ng/dL, or with panhypopituitarism, persistent hyperprolactinemia, or headache/visual field mass effect.

guideline_cutoffs.label: Testosterone therapy safety stop/review: PSA >4 ng/mL, PSA >3 ng/mL in high-risk men without urology review, hematocrit >54%, or MI/stroke within last 6 months
guideline_cutoffs.criteria_text: Endocrine Society recommends against starting testosterone therapy with prostate cancer risk findings including PSA >4 ng/mL or >3 ng/mL in high-risk men without urologic evaluation, elevated hematocrit; trials defined erythrocytosis as hematocrit >54%, and MI/stroke within 6 months is a contraindication.

children.action: Treat reversible causes such as obesity, sleep apnea, medications, opioids/anabolic steroid withdrawal, systemic illness, pituitary disease, or hyperprolactinemia.; Use testosterone therapy only for confirmed symptomatic testosterone deficiency without contraindications and with monitoring of symptoms, testosterone level, hematocrit, PSA/prostate risk, and adverse effects.; Use fertility-preserving approaches such as gonadotropins or selective estrogen receptor modulators under specialist care when conception is desired.; Refer severe secondary hypogonadism, pituitary mass symptoms, very low testosterone with low/normal gonadotropins, infertility, or testicular mass urgently.; Fertility plans change away from exogenous testosterone; pituitary signs trigger MRI/endocrine referral.; Unstable physiology, endocrine crisis, pregnancy-critical medication issue, or dangerous electrolyte/glucose abnormality changes disposition to urgent monitored care.; Discordant or borderline endocrine tests should be confirmed and interpreted with timing, assay, medication, pregnancy, and acute illness context before irreversible treatment.

parallel_actions: Treat reversible causes such as obesity, sleep apnea, medications, opioids/anabolic steroid withdrawal, systemic illness, pituitary disease, or hyperprolactinemia.

Use testosterone therapy only for confirmed symptomatic testosterone deficiency without contraindications and with monitoring of symptoms, testosterone level, hematocrit, PSA/prostate risk, and adverse effects.
Use fertility-preserving approaches such as gonadotropins or selective estrogen receptor modulators under specialist care when conception is desired.
Refer severe secondary hypogonadism, pituitary mass symptoms, very low testosterone with low/normal gonadotropins, infertility, or testicular mass urgently.
Treat unstable presentations first using the urgent escalation rule for Hypogonadism (Low Testosterone in Men); draw time-sensitive labs only if doing so does not delay stabilization.
Fertility plans change away from exogenous testosterone; pituitary signs trigger MRI/endocrine referral.
Unstable physiology, endocrine crisis, pregnancy-critical medication issue, or dangerous electrolyte/glucose abnormality changes disposition to urgent monitored care.
Discordant or borderline endocrine tests should be confirmed and interpreted with timing, assay, medication, pregnancy, and acute illness context before irreversible treatment.

guideline_cutoffs.label: Male hypogonadism pituitary imaging trigger: severe secondary hypogonadism with serum testosterone <150 ng/dL, persistent hyperprolactinemia, panhypopituitarism, or mass effect
guideline_cutoffs.criteria_text: Endocrine Society suggests pituitary imaging when severe secondary hypogonadism is present, for example serum testosterone <150 ng/dL, or with panhypopituitarism, persistent hyperprolactinemia, or headache/visual field mass effect.
guideline_cutoffs.source_section: Figure 1 diagnostic evaluation

guideline_cutoffs.label: Testosterone therapy safety stop/review: PSA >4 ng/mL, PSA >3 ng/mL in high-risk men without urology review, hematocrit >54%, or MI/stroke within last 6 months
guideline_cutoffs.criteria_text: Endocrine Society recommends against starting testosterone therapy with prostate cancer risk findings including PSA >4 ng/mL or >3 ng/mL in high-risk men without urologic evaluation, elevated hematocrit; trials defined erythrocytosis as hematocrit >54%, and MI/stroke within 6 months is a contraindication.
guideline_cutoffs.source_section: Treatment contraindications and systematic review harms

Hypogonadism (Low Testosterone in Men) danger feature or high-risk cutoff: New severe headache/vision loss, very low T with low/normal LH/FSH, testicular mass, infertility priority.; Hemodynamic instability, altered mental status, severe electrolyte/glucose abnormality, arrhythmia, airway compromise, or vision threat requires urgent escalation.; Positive pregnancy with pain/bleeding, rapid virilization, tumor-range androgens, severe abnormal bleeding, visual symptoms/headache, testicular mass, hard breast mass, or priapism.; Measure blood pressure; <150 ng/dL; >4 ng/mL; >3 ng/mL; Fertility plans change away from exogenous testosterone; pituitary signs trigger MRI/endocrine referral.

Male total testosterone should be early morning and repeated; lower limit often around 264-300 ng/dL depending assay.; Important mimics/exclusions: pregnancy, hypothalamic/pituitary disease, thyroid disease, hyperprolactinemia, PCOS, androgen-secreting tumor, medication or substance effects, primary gonadal failure, menopause/POI, and structural pelvic or testicular disease.

clinical_cutoff_criteria.label: Male hypogonadism confirmation: symptoms plus repeat fasting morning total testosterone below harmonized 264 ng/dL lower limit; free T if TT 200-400 ng/dL or SHBG altered
clinical_cutoff_criteria.criteria_text: Endocrine Society recommends diagnosing hypogonadism only with symptoms/signs and consistently low morning testosterone; CDC-harmonized lower limit is 264 ng/dL, and free testosterone is recommended when TT is near the lower limit such as 200-400 ng/dL or SHBG is altered.
data_needed: two fasting morning total testosterone values

clinical_cutoff_criteria.label: Male hypogonadism pituitary imaging trigger: severe secondary hypogonadism with serum testosterone <150 ng/dL, persistent hyperprolactinemia, panhypopituitarism, or mass effect
clinical_cutoff_criteria.criteria_text: Endocrine Society suggests pituitary imaging when severe secondary hypogonadism is present, for example serum testosterone <150 ng/dL, or with panhypopituitarism, persistent hyperprolactinemia, or headache/visual field mass effect.

clinical_cutoff_criteria.label: Testosterone therapy safety stop/review: PSA >4 ng/mL, PSA >3 ng/mL in high-risk men without urology review, hematocrit >54%, or MI/stroke within last 6 months
clinical_cutoff_criteria.criteria_text: Endocrine Society recommends against starting testosterone therapy with prostate cancer risk findings including PSA >4 ng/mL or >3 ng/mL in high-risk men without urologic evaluation, elevated hematocrit; trials defined erythrocytosis as hematocrit >54%, and MI/stroke within 6 months is a contraindication.
clinical_cutoff_criteria.source_section: Treatment contraindications and systematic review harms

clinical_cutoff_review.review_note: Cutoff rows were added because the prior module evidence did not expose enough explicit guideline thresholds for pathway traversal.
